title: Reflection should not be vulnerable to injection attacks
description: Reflection injections occur in a web application when it retrieves data from a user or a third-party service and fully or partially uses it to inspect, load or invoke a component by name. If an application uses a reflection method in a way that is vulnerable to injections, it is exposed to attacks that aim to achieve remote code execution on the server’s website. A user with malicious intent exploits this by carefully crafting a string involving symbols such as class methods, that will help them change the initial reflection logic to an impactful malicious one.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
Assembly assembly = Assembly.LoadFrom(userInput); // User-controlled assembly loading
Type type = assembly.GetType(userControlledTypeName);
Type type = Assembly.GetAssembly(typeof(Program)).GetType(userInput); // Injection via type name

conditions: Contains reflection API call (Type.GetType, Assembly.LoadFrom, GetMethod, etc.)
Uses variable that appears to be user input (input, userInput, request, etc.)
No validation check present before reflection call

logic.reasoning: Detects reflection API calls that use potentially user-controlled input without validation. Identifies patterns where variables with names suggesting external input (userInput, request, param) are passed to reflection methods like Type.GetType, Assembly.LoadFrom, etc. Excludes cases with hardcoded strings, validation checks, and safe contexts like logging or testing.
